title: Suicide: A Study in Sociology
description: Suicide: A Study in Sociology — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "自杀论" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: Table of Contents
memes.description: Table of contents for Le Suicide (1897)

memes.label: Adolph Wagner
memes.description: German economist and statistician (1835–1917), known for his pioneering work on the statistical regularity (Gesetzmässigkeit) of seemingly voluntary human actions, whose empirical methods Durkheim extensively cited in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Adolphe Quetelet
memes.description: Belgian astronomer, mathematician, and statistician (1796–1874), founder of moral statistics and the concept of the "average man" (homme moyen), whose theory of social regularity Durkheim systematically critiqued and refined in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Alexander von Oettingen
memes.description: Baltic German Lutheran theologian (1827-1905) whose pioneering work in moral statistics — Die Moralstatistik (1868, 3rd ed. 1882) — provided Durkheim with extensive empirical data on suicide rates by religion, occupation, and education in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Alexandre Lacassagne
memes.description: French criminologist (1843–1924), founder of the Lyon School of criminology, who proposed an inverse relationship between suicide and property crime — a thesis Durkheim systematically refuted through statistical analysis in Livre II of Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Alfred Legoyt
memes.description: French statistician and demographer (1812–1885), author of Le suicide ancien et moderne (1881), whose extensive suicide data compilation provided Durkheim with crucial empirical evidence across multiple dimensions of suicide analysis.

memes.label: Altruistic Suicide
memes.description: Suicide resulting from excessively strong social integration — the individual is so absorbed into the group that self-sacrifice becomes a social duty.

memes.label: André-Michel Guerry
memes.description: French lawyer and statistician (1802–1866), a founder of moral statistics (statistique morale), whose systematic mapping of suicide rates across French départements provided Durkheim with crucial empirical evidence for the social determination of suicide in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Anomic Suicide
memes.description: A type of suicide resulting from the breakdown of normative regulation — a state of anomie in which individual desires, no longer constrained by collective moral authority, outrun any possibility of satisfaction (Durkheim, 1897, Livre II, Ch. V).

memes.label: Anomie
memes.description: Durkheim's concept of normative deregulation — the breakdown of social norms that regulate individual desires, identified as a primary social cause of suicide and a chronic condition of modern industrial societies.

memes.label: Bourdin
memes.description: French physician, author of Du suicide considéré comme maladie (1845), who advanced the most uncompromising version of the psychiatric theory of suicide — arguing that suicide was always a distinct disease entity — a thesis Durkheim systematically refuted at the outset of Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Cesare Lombroso
memes.description: Italian criminologist and physician (1835–1909), founder of the Italian school of criminal anthropology, whose biological-determinist theory of crime and suicide Durkheim systematically critiqued in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Collective Life and Suicide
memes.description: Durkheim's theory that the intensity of collective life (vie collective) directly governs the suicide rate — stronger collective life protects, while weakened collective life elevates suicide.

memes.label: Corre
memes.description: French naval physician and criminologist, author of Crime et suicide (Paris, 1891) and Le délit et le suicide à Brest (1890), whose statistical data on the relationship between crime and suicide across French departments was cited extensively by Durkheim in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Divorce and Suicide
memes.description: Durkheim's analysis (1897) of the relationship between divorce rates and suicide rates, demonstrating that the institution of divorce weakens marital regulation and produces conjugal anomie, particularly affecting men.

memes.label: E. Lisle
memes.description: French physician, author of Du suicide, statistique, médecine, histoire et législation (1856), whose comprehensive statistical survey of suicide — including the landmark finding of only 6 suicides among 9,320 prison deaths — was extensively cited by Durkheim in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Egoistic Suicide
memes.description: Suicide resulting from excessive individuation — the individual is insufficiently integrated into social groups and lacks collective purpose.

memes.label: Emile Durkheim
memes.description: French sociologist (1858-1917), founder of the sociological method and author of Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Enrico Ferri
memes.description: Italian criminologist and sociologist (1856–1929), leading figure of the Italian school of criminology alongside Lombroso, whose theory of the suicide-homicide relationship Durkheim systematically critiqued in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Enrico Morselli
memes.description: Italian psychiatrist and statistician (1852-1929), author of a major statistical study of suicide that Durkheim extensively engaged with and critiqued in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Fatalistic Suicide
memes.description: Suicide resulting from excessive social regulation — the individual is oppressed by inescapable and inflexible norms.

memes.label: Gabriel Tarde
memes.description: French sociologist and criminologist (1843–1904), Durkheim's principal theoretical rival, whose imitation-based theory of social facts and critique of statistical methodology in criminology Durkheim engaged with in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Garrison
memes.description: French legal historian, author of Le suicide en droit romain et en droit français (Toulouse, 1883), Durkheim's principal source for the medieval and early modern legal treatment of suicide — including confiscation, corporal punishment of corpses, and the gradual decriminalization of suicide — in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Geiger
memes.description: German classical scholar, author of Der Selbstmord im klassischen Altertum (Self-murder in Classical Antiquity), Durkheim's primary source for ancient Greek and Roman suicide legislation and burial practices in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Imitation
memes.description: Durkheim's rigorous redefinition (1897) of imitation as a purely psychological phenomenon distinct from social conformity, and his refutation of Gabriel Tarde's claim that imitation is a primary cause of suicide rates.

memes.label: Individuation
memes.description: The historical process by which individuals become increasingly differentiated from the collective — a key driver of egoistic suicide in modern societies.

memes.label: "Introduction"
memes.description: "Comme le mot de suicide revient sans cesse dans le cours de la"

memes.label: Jacques Bertillon
memes.description: French statistician and demographer (1851–1922), whose pioneering work on divorce and suicide rates provided Durkheim with key empirical evidence for the social causation of suicide in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Jean-Baptiste Cazauvieilh
memes.description: French physician and early suicide researcher, author of Du suicide et de l'aliénation mentale (1840), whose Charenton asylum statistics and hereditary suicide data Durkheim extensively analyzed in Le Suicide (1897) while refuting the psychiatric theory of suicide.

memes.label: Jean-Étienne Esquirol
memes.description: French psychiatrist (1772–1840), student of Philippe Pinel, who systematically argued that suicide was always a symptom of mental illness — a thesis Durkheim refuted at length in Livre I of Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Jean-Pierre Falret
memes.description: French psychiatrist (1794–1870), author of De l'hypocondrie et du suicide (1822), who argued that suicide was involuntary and should not be criminally punished — a position Durkheim engaged with critically in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Dr. Leroy
memes.description: French physician and author of Étude sur le suicide et les maladies mentales dans le département de Seine-et-Marne (1870), whose detailed local suicide statistics Durkheim drew upon extensively in Le Suicide (1897) to demonstrate the social determination of suicide rates.

memes.label: "Livre I, CHAPITRE I"
memes.description: "Le suicide et les états psychopathiques[11]."

memes.label: "Livre I, CHAPITRE II"
memes.description: "Le suicide et les états psychologiques normaux. La race. L'hérédité."

memes.label: "Livre I, CHAPITRE III"
memes.description: "Le suicide et les facteurs cosmiques[84]."

memes.label: "LIVRE II, CHAPITRE PREMIER"
memes.description: "Méthode pour les déterminer."

memes.description: "Observons d'abord la manière dont les différentes confessions"

memes.description: "Le suicide égoïste (Suite)."

memes.label: Louis Brierre de Boismont
memes.description: French alienist (1797-1881) whose clinical study Du suicide et de la folie-suicide (1856) — based on 4,595 suicide case files from Paris (1834-1843) — provided the largest clinical dataset on suicide in the nineteenth century and a critical empirical foundation for Durkheim's Le Suicide.

memes.label: Lunier
memes.description: French physician and psychiatrist (1822–1885), author of De la production et de la consommation des boissons alcooliques en France (1877), whose departmental statistics on alcohol-related mental illness were used by Durkheim in Le Suicide (1897) to test the claim that alcoholism was a primary cause of suicide.

memes.label: Georg von Mayr
memes.description: German statistician and political economist (1841–1897), author of Die Gesetzmässigkeit im Gesellschaftsleben (1877) and Selbstmordstatistik (1895), whose statistical analyses of suicide in Bavaria — particularly regarding grain prices, sex differences, and confessional variation — were cited by Durkheim in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Mental Alienation and Suicide
memes.description: Durkheim's systematic refutation (1897) of the psychiatric claim that suicide is always a manifestation of mental illness (aliénation mentale), demonstrating through statistical argument that suicide rates vary independently of insanity rates.

memes.label: Paul Broca
memes.description: French anatomist and anthropologist (1824–1880), founder of the Société d'Anthropologie de Paris, whose racial classification of French populations — based on cephalic index and craniometric criteria — was critically examined by Durkheim in Le Suicide (1897) during his refutation of racial theories of suicide.

memes.label: Paul Moreau de Tours
memes.description: French psychiatrist and alienist (1844–1908), author of De la contagion du suicide (1871), who argued that suicidal contagion only affected individuals with strong organic predisposition — a theory Durkheim cited and critiqued in his refutation of imitation-based explanations of suicide in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: "Préface"
memes.description: "Depuis quelque temps, la sociologie est à la mode. Le mot, peu connu et"

memes.label: Professional Groups and Suicide
memes.description: Durkheim's proposal (1897) that revived professional corporations (groupes professionnels) are the institutional solution to anomie and egoistic suicide in modern industrial societies, providing the moral integration and regulation that religion, family, and the State no longer supply.

memes.label: Prosper Despine
memes.description: French psychiatrist (1812–1892), author of De la contagion morale (1870) and Psychologie naturelle, who contributed early statistical evidence on the inverse geographical distribution of suicide and violent crime in France — findings later seized upon by the Italian criminological school in Le Suicide (1897).

memes.label: Social Environment and Suicide
memes.description: Durkheim's core methodological concept that the suicide rate is determined by the social environment (milieu social), not by individual-level biological or psychological factors.

memes.label: Social Fact
memes.description: Durkheim's foundational methodological concept — a category of facts that are collective, external to individuals, and exert coercive power over individual behavior.

memes.label: Social Integration
memes.description: The degree to which individuals are bound to collective life — Durkheim's key protective factor against egoistic suicide.

memes.label: Social Regulation
memes.description: The normative force that sets limits on individual desires — one of two key axes of Durkheim's suicide typology.

memes.label: Suicide
memes.description: Core concept in Émile Durkheim's 1897 sociological study — suicide defined as a social fact with a four-type typology (egoistic, altruistic, anomic, fatalistic) determined by collective social forces rather than individual psychology.

memes.label: Suicide and Homicide
memes.description: Durkheim's analysis (1897) of the relationship between suicide and homicide, refuting the Italian criminological school's claim of a universal inverse correlation and demonstrating that both vary with social integration rather than individual psychological predisposition.

memes.label: Suicide Rate
memes.description: The annual number of suicides per unit population — Durkheim's key empirical measure that demonstrates suicide as a social fact.

memes.label: Suicidogenic Current
memes.description: The collective force or social current that impels individuals toward suicide — Durkheim's metaphor for the social determination of individual acts.
